title: Attention Is All You Need
text: This paper introduces the Transformer architecture and shows that self-attention can replace recurrence and convolutions for sequence modeling. The model relies on multi-head attention, positional encodings, and feed-forward blocks to capture long-range dependencies. It demonstrates strong results on machine translation with significantly improved training parallelism.

title: Attention Is All You Need
text: Transformers enable scalable contextual reasoning over long passages, which is essential for retrieval-augmented generation. The attention mechanism also motivates dense embedding models used for semantic search. Many modern retrievers and generators inherit architectural ideas from this work.

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers
text: BERT pre-trains deep bidirectional Transformers using masked language modeling and next sentence prediction. The resulting representations transfer well to many NLP tasks with minimal task-specific architecture changes. The work established a new baseline for contextual embeddings.

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers
text: BERT-based encoders inspired dense retrieval and re-ranking systems that score relevance using contextualized representations. Cross-encoder and bi-encoder retrievers are often built on BERT or its descendants. The work also popularized large-scale pretraining for better semantic matching.

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
text: GPT-3 scales autoregressive Transformers to 175B parameters and demonstrates strong few-shot and zero-shot performance across many tasks. The paper highlights prompt-based learning as a primary interface for capabilities. It also documents limitations in reliability, bias, and factuality.

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
text: Large generators can synthesize across retrieved evidence but remain prone to hallucination without grounding. GPT-3 popularized prompt engineering and context construction practices that are central to RAG systems. It also motivated evaluation of faithfulness and attribution.

title: GPT-4 Technical Report
text: The GPT-4 report describes model scaling, safety work, and broad capability gains across reasoning, coding, and vision tasks. The report emphasizes evaluation, risk mitigation, and the limitations of benchmark-only measurement. It stresses the importance of alignment and system-level controls.

title: GPT-4 Technical Report
text: The report reinforces the need for grounding, transparency, and tool-based workflows when deploying assistants. It motivates traceability and prompt auditing in production. These ideas translate to RAG systems that expose retrieval traces and citations.

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways
text: PaLM scales Transformer language models using the Pathways system and explores scaling laws at the 540B parameter range. The model shows strong performance on reasoning, multilingual tasks, and code generation. The paper also investigates data quality and training stability at scale.

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways
text: The work highlights how scaling improves reasoning but does not remove the need for grounding. RAG systems often pair such models with retrieval to improve factual accuracy. The paper informs expectations about model behavior and prompt sensitivity.

title: Llama 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models
text: Llama 2 describes a family of open foundation models with supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning for chat. The paper focuses on data curation, alignment, and evaluation for safety and helpfulness. It provides evidence that open models can be competitive when tuned carefully.

title: Llama 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models
section: Why it matters for product teams
text: The paper underscores the importance of high-quality data pipelines and evaluation for assistant behavior. It also highlights safety tradeoffs that impact deployment. RAG systems can use such models as generation backends when paired with strong retrieval and provenance.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP
text: RAG combines a neural retriever with a seq2seq generator, conditioning generation on retrieved documents. It introduces end-to-end training and compares different retrieval conditioning strategies. The approach improves factual accuracy on open-domain QA tasks.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP
section: Why it matters for this product
text: RAG formalizes the idea of grounding generation in retrieved evidence. It motivates hybrid retrieval and transparent citations to reduce hallucinations. The architecture is the conceptual backbone for research assistants over technical corpora.

title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
text: DPR introduces dense bi-encoder retrieval for open-domain QA and shows strong gains over sparse baselines. The retriever encodes questions and passages into a shared embedding space and uses vector similarity for retrieval. The work also provides large-scale datasets for evaluation.

title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
section: Why it matters for hybrid search
text: Dense retrieval captures semantic similarity that sparse methods miss, but can struggle with exact term matching. DPR is often paired with BM25 or rerankers to improve recall. Hybrid pipelines build on the strengths of both approaches.

title: REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
text: REALM integrates retrieval into language model pre-training, learning to retrieve latent knowledge during masked language modeling. The model updates retriever parameters jointly with the language model. It demonstrates improved factual recall without increasing model size.

title: REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
text: The paper shows retrieval can be learned as part of the model pipeline, reinforcing the idea that external knowledge is essential. It highlights the interplay between retriever quality and generation accuracy. These insights inform modern RAG system design.

title: Fusion-in-Decoder: Generating Answers with Retrieved Passages
text: FiD encodes each retrieved passage independently and fuses them in the decoder through attention. This allows the model to scale to many passages while preserving passage-level evidence. It improves performance on open-domain QA benchmarks.

title: Fusion-in-Decoder: Generating Answers with Retrieved Passages
text: FiD benefits from well-structured passages, which makes chunking and section-aware splitting critical. It also motivates the use of multiple retrieved chunks rather than a single long context. The architecture inspired multi-document attention strategies.

title: ColBERT: Efficient and Effective Passage Search
text: ColBERT uses contextualized token embeddings and late interaction to improve retrieval quality while keeping efficiency manageable. It indexes token embeddings and performs maximum similarity matching for query tokens. The model bridges sparse and dense retrieval benefits.

title: ColBERT: Efficient and Effective Passage Search
section: Why it matters for hybrid retrieval
text: ColBERT demonstrates that token-level interactions can recover lexical precision while maintaining semantic understanding. It provides a conceptual alternative to pure vector similarity and informs score fusion strategies. The approach highlights the importance of matching granularity.

title: SPLADE: Sparse Lexical and Expansion Model
text: SPLADE learns sparse representations via transformer-based expansions, enabling lexical matching with learned term weights. The model provides interpretability and compatibility with inverted index retrieval. It shows strong performance on BEIR while preserving sparse efficiency.

title: SPLADE: Sparse Lexical and Expansion Model
text: SPLADE offers a middle ground between BM25 and dense embeddings, suggesting additional hybrid strategies. It shows how retrieval can remain interpretable while benefiting from deep models. These ideas influence modern hybrid retrieval pipelines.

title: HyDE: Hypothetical Document Embeddings for Zero-Shot Retrieval
text: HyDE generates a hypothetical document for a query and embeds that synthetic text for retrieval. This can improve zero-shot retrieval when the original query is short or underspecified. The method uses an LLM as a query expansion tool.

title: HyDE: Hypothetical Document Embeddings for Zero-Shot Retrieval
section: Why it matters for query understanding
text: The approach highlights the value of query rewriting and expansion in retrieval pipelines. It also suggests that LLMs can help bridge vocabulary mismatch between queries and documents. RAG systems can incorporate similar techniques for hard queries.

title: The Probabilistic Relevance Framework: BM25 and Extensions
text: BM25 formalizes probabilistic term weighting for document ranking based on term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length. It remains a strong baseline for lexical retrieval. The framework also motivates variations used in modern search systems.

title: The Probabilistic Relevance Framework: BM25 and Extensions
section: Why it matters for hybrid search
text: BM25 is still competitive for exact match and keyword-heavy queries. Combining BM25 with dense retrieval improves recall and robustness. Hybrid systems often normalize BM25 scores and blend them with dense similarity.

title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
text: ReAct interleaves reasoning traces with action steps, enabling models to call tools and incorporate observations. It improves task completion on knowledge-intensive and multi-hop tasks. The paper provides a prompting format that alternates thought and action.

title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
text: ReAct demonstrates that explicit tool usage improves grounding and transparency. RAG systems can borrow the idea of trace steps that show retrieval and synthesis. It also motivates exposing intermediate actions in a debug panel.

title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
text: Chain-of-Thought prompting encourages models to generate intermediate reasoning steps and improves performance on multi-step problems. The paper shows that few-shot examples can unlock reasoning behavior without additional training. It emphasizes careful prompt design.

title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
section: Why it matters for grounded QA
text: CoT can improve coherence, but it can also amplify hallucinations if not grounded. RAG systems pair reasoning with evidence to keep outputs factual. It also motivates structured prompts that separate evidence and conclusions.

title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
text: Tree of Thoughts explores structured search over reasoning paths, allowing models to branch, evaluate, and backtrack. The approach improves performance on tasks that benefit from exploration. It introduces a general framework for controlling reasoning.

title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
section: Why it matters for agentic RAG
text: ToT suggests that retrieval can be integrated into multi-branch reasoning where evidence is gathered per branch. It supports the idea of exposing reasoning traces and scoring alternatives. The approach also highlights the need for evaluation and pruning strategies.

title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
text: Toolformer introduces a self-supervised approach to teach LLMs when and how to call tools. The model augments training data with tool call traces and learns to insert API calls in generation. It demonstrates improvements on tasks that require external knowledge or computation.

title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
text: The paper shows that tool usage can be learned and not just prompted. Retrieval can be treated as a tool whose calls are made explicit in the trace. This motivates clear logging and reproducible tool invocation in assistant systems.

title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
text: Self-consistency samples multiple reasoning paths and aggregates answers to improve reliability. It reduces the variance of a single chain-of-thought output. The technique improves accuracy on arithmetic and commonsense benchmarks.

title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
text: The paper highlights the need to reason about uncertainty and variance in model outputs. RAG systems can extend the idea by comparing answers derived from different retrieved contexts. It suggests evaluation strategies that consider multiple generations.

title: HotpotQA: A Dataset for Diverse, Explainable Multi-Hop Question Answering
text: HotpotQA introduces multi-hop questions that require combining information from multiple documents. It provides supporting facts for explainability and encourages reasoning across sources. The dataset is widely used for evaluation of retrieval and reasoning.

title: HotpotQA: A Dataset for Diverse, Explainable Multi-Hop Question Answering
text: Multi-hop QA stresses retrieval pipelines by requiring multiple relevant chunks. It also motivates trace outputs that show which sources were combined. RAG assistants often need to demonstrate similar evidence chaining.

title: Natural Questions: A Benchmark for Question Answering Research
text: Natural Questions provides real user queries with annotated answers from Wikipedia. It includes long and short answer spans and focuses on open-domain retrieval. The dataset captures the ambiguity and noise of real-world queries.

title: Natural Questions: A Benchmark for Question Answering Research
text: The dataset highlights the need for robust retrieval and answer synthesis under ambiguous inputs. It informs evaluation pipelines that separate retrieval errors from generation errors. RAG systems can leverage similar datasets for benchmarking.

title: BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Information Retrieval
text: BEIR aggregates diverse IR datasets and evaluates retrieval models in a zero-shot setting. It highlights performance gaps between dense retrievers and lexical baselines across domains. The benchmark encourages robust, domain-general retrieval.

title: BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Information Retrieval
text: BEIR demonstrates that no single retriever dominates across all domains. Hybrid approaches and reranking can improve performance in difficult settings. Evaluation across BEIR can guide retrieval configuration and embeddings selection.

title: MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark
text: MTEB evaluates text embedding models across a wide range of tasks, including retrieval, clustering, and classification. It provides a standardized view of embedding performance beyond a single dataset. The benchmark also captures tradeoffs between model size and accuracy.

title: MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark
text: Embedding choice impacts retrieval quality and cost. MTEB provides evidence to compare embedding models before integrating them into RAG systems. It also highlights how embeddings behave under domain shift.

title: RAGAS: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text: RAGAS defines metrics for faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision, and context recall in RAG systems. It provides a framework for evaluating retrieval and generation quality together. The approach relies on LLM-based judgment and structured datasets.

title: RAGAS: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval-Augmented Generation
section: Why it matters for this repo
text: RAGAS metrics help identify whether failures come from retrieval or generation. They support repeatable evaluation runs and comparison between different retrieval configurations. The metrics are a good fit for the evaluation pipeline in this project.

title: FACTSCORE: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factuality
text: FACTSCORE evaluates factuality by decomposing responses into atomic claims and verifying them against evidence. It emphasizes precise, attributable answers rather than generic summaries. The methodology motivates tighter grounding in generation.

title: FACTSCORE: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factuality
section: Why it matters for grounded answers
text: Fine-grained factuality is a core goal of research assistants. RAG systems can use similar claim checking or citation alignment to improve trust. The paper informs how to design evaluation data for factual correctness.

title: HyDE for Retrieval: Practical Notes
text: This blog post explains HyDE in practical terms and describes how to integrate hypothetical document generation into retrieval pipelines. It discusses query expansion and sampling strategies. The post includes implementation guidance and tradeoffs.

title: HyDE for Retrieval: Practical Notes
text: Operational guidance helps teams implement HyDE without overfitting to single datasets. The post emphasizes the need to measure retrieval gains with evaluation sets. It aligns with the idea of configurable retrieval steps in production.

title: Hybrid Search for RAG
text: The Pinecone hybrid search guide explains how to combine sparse and dense scores for retrieval. It covers normalization, weighting, and evaluation strategies. The post emphasizes that different query types benefit from different retrieval signals.

title: Hybrid Search for RAG
text: Hybrid scoring is a core feature of the product. The guidance supports score normalization and alpha blending of dense and sparse retrieval. It also highlights the need for traceability to debug ranking behavior.

title: Weaviate Hybrid Search Overview
text: Weaviate documents how hybrid search blends keyword and vector signals, including configurable alpha weighting. It describes use cases where lexical matching prevents missed exact terms. The docs also detail score interpretation and practical tuning.

title: Weaviate Hybrid Search Overview
text: Hybrid search works best when teams can observe how each signal impacts ranking. This supports the need for trace steps that display dense and BM25 scores side by side. It also informs default tuning for general usage.

title: RAG Best Practices for Production
text: This guide summarizes best practices for chunking, retrieval, reranking, and grounding. It emphasizes choosing chunk sizes based on document structure and task behavior. The guide also highlights evaluation and monitoring for RAG systems.

title: RAG Best Practices for Production
section: Why it matters for this repo
text: The guidance reinforces section-aware chunking and traceability. It also encourages building evaluation sets early so retrieval changes can be measured. These practices align with the product goals in this repository.

title: LangChain Retrieval QA Concepts
text: LangChain documents common RAG building blocks including loaders, splitters, retrievers, and chains. It describes how to assemble retrieval QA systems with configurable components. The docs emphasize modularity and observability.

title: LangChain Retrieval QA Concepts
text: The modular design aligns with provider abstractions and clean service boundaries. It supports an incremental approach to improving retrieval and generation. The guidance also encourages clear data flow between components.

title: Haystack RAG Pipelines
text: Haystack provides pipeline abstractions for retrieval, ranking, and generation. The documentation shows how to build multi-step flows with retrievers and generators while tracking intermediate outputs. It emphasizes evaluation and experimentation.

title: Haystack RAG Pipelines
text: Pipeline-oriented systems make it easier to expose trace steps to users. The docs highlight configuration over hardcoded logic, supporting the repo's modular goal. They also reinforce best practices for dataset-driven evaluation.

title: Gemini Embeddings API Overview
text: The Gemini embeddings documentation describes how to generate dense vectors for text, with task-specific settings for retrieval. It explains model selection and request structure. The API supports separate settings for documents and queries.

title: Gemini Embeddings API Overview
section: Why it matters for this stack
text: Gemini embeddings integrate cleanly with the rest of the Gemini ecosystem. Using task-specific embeddings helps improve retrieval quality. The docs inform how to configure and operationalize embeddings for RAG.

title: Reranking for Retrieval Pipelines
text: Cohere describes reranking as a second-stage retrieval step that improves ranking quality by scoring query-document pairs. It highlights accuracy improvements over pure embedding retrieval. The docs emphasize latency tradeoffs and batch evaluation.

title: Reranking for Retrieval Pipelines
section: Why it matters for hybrid retrieval
text: Reranking is a natural extension for hybrid pipelines once candidate sets are retrieved. It can help stabilize rankings and improve citation quality. The documentation underscores the need for evaluation metrics when tuning rerankers.

title: Information Retrieval Metrics: nDCG, MRR, Recall
text: The IR textbook provides foundational definitions for ranking metrics such as nDCG, MRR, and recall. It explains how these metrics capture different aspects of retrieval quality. The chapter includes practical guidance on evaluation methodology.

title: Information Retrieval Metrics: nDCG, MRR, Recall
text: Retrieval metrics help quantify improvements from chunking and score fusion. They also make it possible to compare dense, sparse, and hybrid retrievers. A research assistant should expose these metrics in its eval pipeline.

title: LlamaIndex RAG Concepts
text: LlamaIndex documents common RAG components including loaders, indexes, retrievers, and response synthesizers. It emphasizes data connectors and metadata-driven retrieval. The docs highlight observability and evaluation tools.

title: LlamaIndex RAG Concepts
section: Why it matters for this product
text: Metadata-rich retrieval aligns with the product goal of premium source cards. The docs reinforce clean service boundaries between indexing and generation. These practices help keep the architecture modular and extensible.

title: Evaluating RAG Systems in Practice
text: This article outlines practical strategies for evaluating RAG systems, including retrieval metrics, answer quality, and human review loops. It emphasizes repeatable datasets and experiment tracking. The piece also describes common failure modes such as poor chunking.

title: Evaluating RAG Systems in Practice
section: Why it matters for the roadmap
text: The guidance supports adding evaluation datasets and preserving failed cases for analysis. It also emphasizes experiment logging, which can be added later to the system. It aligns with the goal of a production-like evaluation workflow.

title: Tracing and Observability for LLM Apps
text: LangSmith documents tracing and observability for LLM pipelines, including run graphs and token-level logs. It highlights how traces make it easier to debug retrieval and prompt issues. The system encourages structured metadata for every step.

title: Tracing and Observability for LLM Apps
section: Why it matters for trace UX
text: Trace-friendly tooling shows the value of structured step output. The product can emulate a subset of these ideas with a clear trace panel. It also suggests naming steps consistently and keeping prompt context visible.

title: A Survey of Hybrid Information Retrieval
text: This survey reviews hybrid retrieval systems that combine sparse and dense signals. It outlines score fusion strategies, training methods, and evaluation protocols. The survey also covers open challenges around efficiency and interpretability.

title: A Survey of Hybrid Information Retrieval
section: Why it matters for score fusion
text: Score normalization and weighting are central to hybrid retrieval. The survey provides rationale for explicit fusion rather than ad hoc mixing. It underscores the importance of diagnostics when tuning hybrid parameters.

title: A Survey of Evaluation for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text: The survey reviews evaluation strategies for RAG, including automated metrics, human evaluation, and dataset design. It distinguishes retrieval quality from answer correctness and highlights common pitfalls. The paper also discusses faithfulness and attribution metrics.

title: A Survey of Evaluation for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
section: Why it matters for the eval pipeline
text: A clear evaluation taxonomy helps choose appropriate metrics for different product goals. The survey motivates using both retrieval and generation metrics in batch evaluation. It also encourages preserving failed cases for inspection.

title: Long Context Language Models: A Survey
text: This survey reviews techniques for extending context length, including attention optimizations, retrieval augmentation, and memory mechanisms. It contrasts long-context training with retrieval-based approaches. The paper highlights tradeoffs in latency and accuracy.

title: Long Context Language Models: A Survey
text: Even with long contexts, retrieval remains critical for efficiency and focus. The survey motivates smarter chunking and section-aware selection to avoid overwhelming the model. It also suggests that structured context yields better answer quality.

title: Evaluating Tool-Using Agents
text: This paper proposes evaluation setups for agents that use tools and external knowledge. It introduces metrics for tool selection accuracy and error analysis. The work emphasizes tracing to attribute failure causes.

title: Evaluating Tool-Using Agents
section: Why it matters for trace design
text: Agent evaluation benefits from clear visibility into tool usage. The trace panel in the product can mirror these requirements by showing retrieval candidates and fusion scores. The paper supports a structured, inspectable workflow.

title: Retrieval Evaluation and Testing
text: The evaluation guide discusses building test sets, writing assertions, and running repeatable evals. It emphasizes separating retrieval performance from generation behavior. The guide also highlights the value of storing failed cases.

title: Retrieval Evaluation and Testing
text: A consistent eval pipeline enables faster iteration on retrieval tuning. The guide reinforces the need to capture inputs, context, and outputs for each run. These ideas align with the batch evaluation workflow in this repo.

title: Google RAG Pattern Guide
text: Google Cloud outlines a reference architecture for RAG systems including ingestion, indexing, retrieval, and generation. The guide emphasizes data pipelines, security, and observability. It includes practical advice on chunking and vector storage.

title: Google RAG Pattern Guide
text: The guide supports a modular design with explicit ingestion and retrieval stages. It also highlights the need for monitoring and clear configuration management. These recommendations map to the app structure in this repository.

title: Anthropic RAG and Retrieval Guidance
text: Anthropic documentation discusses building retrieval workflows, including prompt structure and citation practices. It emphasizes careful context assembly and clear system instructions. The guidance is oriented toward grounded responses.

title: Anthropic RAG and Retrieval Guidance
text: Prompt structure is critical for enforceable citations. The recommendations support the system prompt used in this project. Even with Gemini as the backend, similar prompt discipline applies.

title: Gemini RAG Guidance
text: The Gemini grounding documentation explains how to structure prompts for grounded answers and how to integrate retrieval into Gemini workflows. It covers tool calling, citations, and safety considerations. The docs also provide reference architectures.

title: Gemini RAG Guidance
section: Why it matters for this product
text: Gemini-specific guidance ensures the prompt and retrieval flow remain compatible with the chosen provider. It supports a clean provider abstraction that can still surface grounding requirements. The guidance also reinforces the importance of trace visibility.

title: A Survey of Retrieval-Augmented Language Models
text: This survey reviews retrieval-augmented language models, covering architectures, training strategies, and evaluation. It maps the landscape of retrievers, generators, and memory modules. The survey also outlines open challenges such as stale knowledge and latency.

title: A Survey of Retrieval-Augmented Language Models
section: Why it matters for product strategy
text: The survey provides a roadmap of design choices for RAG systems. It supports a modular architecture that can adapt to new retriever or generator types. It also highlights the importance of keeping corpora fresh and well-curated.
